Details: Based on bench-scale studies [2746, 2748, 2749, 2753, 2755, 2756, 2773, 2774, 2780, 2792], UV irradiation seems to be an effective treatment for cylindrospermopsin when combined with an oxidating agent. Various treatment protocols showed a range of removal rates with higher removal rate in tests of titanium dioxide with UV exposures (up to 100 percent) and lower rates in tests with UV alone (8 percent to 90 percent). Effectiveness varied with UV contact time and dose. The contact times in some of these studies were significantly longer than those typically used in full-scale UV treatment of drinking water.
